# Novel method for measuring high-frequency wheel-rail force considering wheelset vibrations

[*Scientific Reports*](/srep)
**volume 15**, Article number: 15044 (2025)
[Cite this article](#citeas)

935 Accesses

[Metrics details](/articles/s41598-025-96212-2/metrics)

### Subjects

## Abstract

This article introduces a groundbreaking approach for accurately gauging high-frequency wheel-rail forces, taking into account the impact of wheelset vibration modes on measurement outcomes. The method relies on lateral and vertical acceleration data from the left and right axle box positions, combined with a finite element model, to determine the system’s frequency response function. Subsequently, it inverts the lateral and vertical wheel rail forces of both wheels. In terms of data processing, we employed the Fourier transform method, abandoning the traditional conversion to power spectral density functions. This improvement ensures that the phase information of the data is preserved, significantly enhancing the reliability of the results. To validate the reliability and accuracy of this method, a comparative study was conducted by establishing a model. Specifically, we conducted in-depth discussions on high-frequency wheel rail forces around 580 Hz. The comparison outcomes indicate a close correlation between the results obtained from SIMPACK’s direct simulation (5.7kN) and the inverse method (5.2kN). These findings robustly support the theoretical accuracy and practical feasibility of this method.

## Introduction

The study of wheel-rail forces in high-speed trains is of the utmost importance and cannot be overstated. These forces are not only directly related to issues such as rail head wear, track defects, and derailments, but also significantly impact the track’s durability and safety[1](#ref-CR1 "Shi, H., Wang, J. & Wu, P. Field measurements of the evolution of wheel wear and vehicle dynamics for high-speed trains. Veh. Syst. Dyn. 56, 1187–1206 (2018)."),[2](#ref-CR2 "Wang, Q., Zeng, J. & Mao, R. Parameters optimum of multiple underframe suspended equipment on high-speed railway vehicle carbody vibration control by using an improved genetic algorithm. J. Vib. Control. 30, 23–24 (2024)."),[3](/articles/s41598-025-96212-2#ref-CR3 "Wang, Q. et al. A diagnostic method of freight wagons hunting performance based on wayside hunting detection system. Measurement 227, 114274 (2024)."). The vertical force of the wheel-rail interaction is primarily influenced by factors such as the wheel’s shape, the rail’s corrugation, rail joints, and line distortion. On the other hand, the lateral force is primarily affected sliding, hunting motion, and curve passing between the wheel and rail[4](/articles/s41598-025-96212-2#ref-CR4 "Wu, H. et al. Fatigue analysis of the gearbox housing in high-speed trains under wheel polygonization using a multibody dynamics algorithm. Eng. Fail. Anal. 100, 351–364 (2019)."),[5](/articles/s41598-025-96212-2#ref-CR5 "Shi, H., Zeng, J. & Guo, J. Disturbance observer-based sliding mode control of active vertical suspension for high-speed rail vehicles. Veh. Syst. Dyn. 62, 2912–2935 (2024)."). These forces are crucial in assessing the risk of rail panel rollover, the stability of vehicle hunting motion, and evaluating derailment safety. So, on the one hand, certain measures should be taken to mitigate the transmission of high-frequency vibrations[6](/articles/s41598-025-96212-2#ref-CR6 "Sharma, S. K. & Anil, K. Ride comfort of a higher speed rail vehicle using a magnetorheological suspension system. Proc. Inst. Mech. Eng. Part K J. Multi Body Dyn. 232, 32–48 (2018)."); on the other hand, the development of wheel-rail force testing technology is also urgently needed.

Currently, there are two common methods for measuring wheel-rail forces: instrumented wheelset[7](#ref-CR7 "Ronasi, H., Johansson, H. & Larsson, F. Identification of wheel–rail contact forces based on strain measurements, an inverse scheme and a finite-element model of the wheel. Proc. Inst. Mech. Eng. Part F J. Rail Rapid Transit 228, 343–354 (2013)."),[8](#ref-CR8 "O’Brien, E., Bowe, C. & Quirke, P. Determination of longitudinal profile of railway track using vehicle-based inertial readings. Proc. Inst. Mech. Eng. Part F J. Rail Rapid Transit 231, 518–534 (2017)."),[9](#ref-CR9 "Ai, C., Qiu, S. & Xu, G. A. Nonballasted rail track slab crack identificationmethod using a level set based active contour model. Comput. Aided Civ. Infrastruct. Eng. 33, 571–584 (2018)."),[10](/articles/s41598-025-96212-2#ref-CR10 "Cazzulani, G., Di, G. & Bionda, S. A new approach for the evaluation and the improvement of the metrological characteristics of an instrumented wheelset for the measure of wheel–rail contact forces. Proc. Inst. Mech. Eng. Part F J. Rail Rapid Transit 231, 381–393 (2017).") and ground measurement methods[11](#ref-CR11 "Jonsson, J., Svensson, E. & Christensen, J. Strain gauge measurement of wheel-rail interaction forces. J. Strain Anal. Eng. Des. 32, 183–191 (1997)."),[12](#ref-CR12 "Bagheri, V., Tehrani, P. & Younesian, D. Optimal strain gauge placement in instrumented wheelset for measuring wheel-rail contact forces. Int. J. Precis. Eng. Manuf. 18, 1519–1527 (2017)."),[13](#ref-CR13 "Li, Y. et al. Continuous output algorithm for wheel-rail force of force measurement rail. J. Traffic Transp. Eng. 11, 36–40 (2011)."),[14](/articles/s41598-025-96212-2#ref-CR14 "Peng, X. et al. Wayside wheel-rail vertical contact force continuous detecting method and its application. Measurement 193, 110975 (2022)."). However, the calibration process of both methods is static and does not account for the dynamic response of the elastomer. Therefore, when the wheelset undergoes elastic vibration, these two methods may not be able to accurately measure the forces. Specifically, in some high frequency vibration scenarios, such as vibrations caused by wheel polygonalization or corrugation, which may reach several hundred hertz, we require further exploration into novel methods for accurately calculating wheel-rail forces.

In the field of high-speed train wheel-rail force measurement, indirect measurement methods have recently gained popularity among researchers. Wei[15](#ref-CR15 "Wei, L. et al. Hunting stability and dynamic stress analysis of a high-speed bogie using elastic-suspended motors as dynamic vibration absorber. Veh. Syst. Dyn. 62, 2332–2354 (2024)."),[16](#ref-CR16 "Wei, L. et al. Safety analysis of high speed trains under cross winds using indirect wheel-rail force measuring method. J. Wind Eng. Ind. Aerodyn. 183, 55–67 (2018)."),[17](#ref-CR17 "Wei, L. et al. Indirect method for wheel–rail force measurement and derailment evaluation. Veh. Syst. Dyn. 52, 1622–1641 (2014)."),[18](/articles/s41598-025-96212-2#ref-CR18 "Wei, L., Zeng, J. & Wu, P. Derailment safety evaluation for railway vehicles based on wheelset model. J. China Railw. Soc. 37, 25–31 (2015).") introduced a method that treats wheelsets as rigid bodies. By analyzing the inertia force and suspension force of the wheelset based on its force state, he derived the lateral and vertical forces acting on the wheel axle. These forces are then translated into observable responses, such as primary displacement and axle box acceleration, making the measurement process straightforward and cost-effective. However, this method remains limited in its application to low-frequency wheel-rail forces, posing a significant constraint. Zhu[19](/articles/s41598-025-96212-2#ref-CR19 "Zhu, T. et al. An inverse dynamics method for railway vehicle systems. Transport 29, 107–114 (2014).") presented a novel non-iterative mathematical model for dynamic load inversion, drawing from dynamic programming and Bellman optimization principles. This model utilizes lateral, vertical, and longitudinal accelerations of the axle box as inputs, enabling the calculation of lateral and vertical wheel-rail loads in reverse. WD et al.[20](/articles/s41598-025-96212-2#ref-CR20 "Wang, W., Niu, L. & Liu, J. Medium and high frequency wheel-rail vertical force soft measurement model based on axle box vertical acceleration. China Railw. Sci. 34, 100–105 (2013).") identified a waveform similarity between measured axle box acceleration and wheel rail force data. They employed spectral analysis and coherence function analysis to determine the transfer functions between wheel rail force and axle box acceleration, ultimately establishing a wheel rail force prediction model based on axle box acceleration. This model’s applicable wheel rail force range extends up to 90 Hz, significantly expanding its practical applications. Lanxiao Bi[21](/articles/s41598-025-96212-2#ref-CR21 "Bi, L. et al. Wayside testing methods for high-frequency vertical wheel-rail impact forces and its applicability. Measurement 151, 107197 (2020).") constructed an accurate numerical track structure model to simulate wheel-rail impact behavior and compared different methods for testing wheel-rail forces to validate their effectiveness. Changrong Yang[22](/articles/s41598-025-96212-2#ref-CR22 "Yang, C. & Tian, G. Wheel-rail force measurement based on wireless LC resonance sensing. IEEE Sens. J. 23, 17470–17479 (2023).") proposes a wireless inductive-capacitive resonance sensing approach for the non-contact measurement of wheel-rail contact forces.

There has been no clear demarcation point for the range of high-frequency wheel-rail forces. The first modal frequency of high-speed train wheelsets is typically around 70–80 Hz. Generally, we consider wheel-rail forces that are close to or higher than this first modal frequency as high-frequency. In other words, when the wheelset experiences modal vibration, it can be considered that there are high-frequency wheel-rail forces. High-frequency wheel-rail forces are relative to low-frequency ones, whose frequency range is much lower than the first modal frequency of the wheelset. In this case, the wheelset can be treated as a rigid body for obtaining wheel-rail forces. Therefore, the acquisition of high-frequency and low-frequency wheel-rail forces is fundamentally different in principle.

Wheel polygonization and rail corrugation are common fault conditions in high-speed trains, and currently, there are no effective means to prevent them. Maintenance can only be carried out by turning the wheels or grinding the rails when the conditions accumulate to a certain extent to ensure normal operation. Therefore, it is essential to study the high-frequency vibrations induced by these conditions. High-frequency wheel-rail force excitations can cause vibration fatigue in critical vehicle components, leading to the failure of many parts well before their design lifespan. There have been numerous cases, such as the fracture of axle box end cover bolts resulting in the detachment of the end cover, and the cracking of bogie frames. The root cause of these issues is that high-frequency wheel-rail forces induce resonance in critical components, accelerating the damage process. Therefore, the research on high-frequency wheel-rail forces has significant practical implications for the life management of critical components and the safe operation of trains.

In summary, The indirect measurement method remains limited in its frequency range, and traditional power spectral density (PSD) analysis, which does not account for phase superposition error, often struggles to handle multiple load inputs effectively. Therefore, the measurement method of high-frequency wheel-rail force deserves further exploration.

## Theoretical method

When examining the wheel rail force of the entire wheelset system, it can be conceptualized as addressing the challenge of multiple load inputs through dynamic response analysis. The crux of determining the system’s response or input load in a vibrational context lies in acquiring the frequency response function of the system. This function offers a precise portrayal of the system’s response characteristics across various frequencies, which is paramount for comprehending and predicting alterations in wheel rail forces.

### Solving the frequency response function based on the PSD method

In the traditional approach, when dealing with the response issues of linear systems under random excitation, the Power Spectral Density (PSD) method is frequently utilized. When a linear system is excited by a harmonic function \({\text{f}}({\text{t}})={{\text{f}}\_0}\sin \omega {\text{t}}\) with a specific frequency \(\omega \), its response exhibits corresponding harmonic wave characteristics of the same frequency. It is crucial to note, however, that there may exist a phase difference \(\varphi \) between the response and excitation, which can be denoted as\({\text{y}}({\text{t}})={y\_0}\sin (\omega t - \varphi )\). By focusing solely on the amplitude ratio \({y\_0}/{y\_{\text{f}}}\) and phase angle \(\varphi \), we can effectively describe the transmission characteristics of the system. To facilitate expression, we often represent these two parameters using complex numbers, which constitute the transfer function \(H(\omega )\). The transfer function offers a comprehensive description of the system’s response behavior. The modulus of the function represents the amplitude ratio between the system’s output and input functions, while the ratio of its imaginary and real parts indicates the phase angle’s tangent value. Namely:

Among:

It is worth emphasizing that in the aforementioned formula, amplitude serves as a general term that can encompass the amplitude of displacement, vibration velocity, or vibration acceleration. Depending on the specific context of amplitude, the corresponding value of \(H(\omega )\) may vary. In this article, our primary focus is on the load acceleration response, hence the input is force load and the output is acceleration response. This implies that in practical applications, appropriate amplitude and corresponding \(H(\omega )\) values must be chosen based on the specific circumstances.

According to the plural notation \({e^{j\omega t}}=\cos \omega t+j\sin \omega t\), the input and output function can be expressed as:

In the formula, \({\text{f}}(\omega )\) represents the frequency domain representation of the input load \(f(t)\), while \({\text{y}}(\omega )\) signifies the frequency domain representation of the output response \({\text{y}}({\text{t}})\).

Assuming that the system is excited by a certain load \(f(t)\), the equation of motion is:

Put formulas ([4](/articles/s41598-025-96212-2#Equ4)) and ([5](/articles/s41598-025-96212-2#Equ5)) into the equation of motion ([6](/articles/s41598-025-96212-2#Equ6)) to obtain:

The above result is the frequency response function of the output as the load-displacement, and the frequency response function of the load-vibration speed and load-acceleration are respectively:

Upon acquiring the frequency response function, it is important to note the following relationships for a single input function *f*(*t*) with a power spectral density of \({S\_f}(\omega )\) and an output function *y*(*t*) with a power spectral density of \({S\_y}(\omega )\):

For multiple incoherent input loads, obtain the power spectral density of the response function:

In the formula, \({S\_{fr}}(\omega )\)is the power spectral density of the r-th input; \({H\_r}(\omega )\) is the frequency response function of the r-th input.

In summary, when addressing load input based on vibration response, it is essential to convert vibration response data into power spectral density. Similarly, the input load obtained from the solution is also presented in the form of power spectral density. Subsequently, these data are converted back into time-domain data. While this method is widely utilized, its frequency domain phase information is disregarded when expressing the load in PSD. When dealing with multi-load input problems, this omission can lead to significant errors.

### Solving the response function based on the FFT method

To address the phase issue associated with multiple load inputs, this study employs a calculation approach based on Fourier transform (FFT) rather than traditional PSD processing methods. This is because the load frequency domain information obtained via FFT incorporates valuable phase information that cannot be captured through PSD processing techniques. The wheelset system, as a representative multi-load input system, features multiple load input points. This section aims to investigate the utilization of the FFT method instead of the PSD method to calculate acceleration response under conditions of multiple load inputs.

When dealing with multi-load input problems, according to the superposition principle, the response function \(y(\omega )\) can be solved using formula ([12](/articles/s41598-025-96212-2#Equ12)), followed by the retrieval of time-domain data \(y(t)\) via inverse transformation. This approach offers a more precise and comprehensive solution, particularly when considering phase information.

In the formula, \({f\_{\text{r}}}(\omega )\)is the frequency domain data of the r-th input load; \({{\text{H}}\_{\text{r}}}(\omega )\)is the load acceleration frequency response function of the r-th load; \({\text{y}}(\omega )\) is the frequency domain data of the output response.

The load acceleration frequency response function formula ([9](/articles/s41598-025-96212-2#Equ9)) can be further reformulated as formula ([13](/articles/s41598-025-96212-2#Equ13)). It is worth noting that the frequency response function is complex, encompassing both real and imaginary components. This implies that the frequency response function not only provides amplitude information but also incorporates phase information, enabling a more comprehensive description of the system’s dynamic characteristics.

Let the real part \(A(\omega )=\frac{{M{\omega ^4} - K{\omega ^2}}}{{{{(K - M{\omega ^2})}^2}+{{(C\omega )}^2}}}\), and the imaginary part \(B(\omega )=\frac{{C{\omega ^3}}}{{{{(K - M{\omega ^2})}^2}+{{(C\omega )}^2}}}\). The real and imaginary parts of this frequency sound function can be obtained using a finite element model.

In the approach for handling input loads, we begin by considering the input scenario of a single load \(f(n)\). In the experimental setup, all load signals are discrete. We assume that the data length of these signals is *N*z, \({\text{f}}(n)\) is the real signal, and the imaginary part is 0. Given these conditions, we can obtain their FFT results as follows:

The load is also in plural form after FFT conversion, let:

So the acceleration response under load can be obtained by plural operatios:

Then the Fourier inverse transform (IFFT) is applied to obtain the time domain signal of acceleration response

When there are multiple external loads, let the total number of loads be *k*, and the FFT of the *i*-th external load *fi(n)* can be expressed as:

Given that distinct loads can excite modes of varying orders, resulting in a response at the desired location, it follows that each external load *fi(n)* is associated with a load acceleration frequency response function *H**i*(ω) regarding the output point position. Consequently, the acceleration frequency domain signal at the focal position can be expressed as:

Finally, combined with formula ([17](/articles/s41598-025-96212-2#Equ17)), the time domain signal of acceleration can be obtained.

The aforementioned description pertains to the general scenario of vibration systems. For the axle box wheelset system studied in this article, we primarily consider lateral and vertical forces, disregarding the impact of longitudinal forces on the wheel rail. Consequently, the system is exposed to four input loads, comprising lateral and vertical forces exerted on both wheels. We posit that the discrete time-domain data for the lateral and vertical forces acting on the left wheel are *f**LL*(*n*) and *f**LV*(*n*), respectively, while the corresponding time-domain data for the right wheel are *f**RL*(*n*) and *f**RV*(*n*), respectively. By applying formula ([14](/articles/s41598-025-96212-2#Equ14)), the plural frequency-domain signals of these forces after Fourier transform are *F**LL*(*ω*), *F**LV*(*ω*), *F**RL*(*ω*), and *F**RV*(*ω*), respectively. At the position of the left axle box, the discrete time-domain signals of lateral and vertical acceleration are \({a\_{LL}}(n)\) and \({a\_{L{\text{V}}}}(n)\), respectively. Similarly, at the position of the right axle box, the discrete time-domain signals of these two accelerations are \({a\_{RL}}(n)\) and \({a\_{RV}}(n)\), respectively. Applying formula ([14](/articles/s41598-025-96212-2#Equ14)), the complex frequency-domain signals of these acceleration signals after Fourier transform are \({a\_{LL}}(\omega )\), \({a\_{LV}}(\omega )\), \({a\_{RL}}(\omega )\), and \({a\_{RV}}(\omega )\), respectively. It should be noted that the acceleration at each response point is the result of the combined action of four loads: *F**LL*(*ω*), *F**LV*(*ω*), *F**RL*(*ω*), and *F**RV*(*ω*). Consequently, the lateral and vertical accelerations of the left and right axle boxes can be expressed as follows using formula ([19](/articles/s41598-025-96212-2#Equ19)):

In the aforementioned equation, the subscript of the load acceleration frequency response function is denoted by the initial two letters indicating the load type and the final two letters indicating the response position. For instance, \({{\text{H}}\_{{\text{LV}}-{\text{RL}}}}(\omega )\) represents the frequency response function of the left vertical wheel rail force on the lateral acceleration of the right axle box. All these frequency response functions can be accurately computed using finite element models. The time-domain data for the lateral and vertical accelerations of the left and right axle boxes, namely\({a\_{LL}}(n)\) \({a\_{LV}}(n)\), \({a\_{RL}}(n)\) and \({a\_{RV}}(n)\), can be obtained through line tests. By utilizing Fast Fourier Transform (FFT), these data can be transformed into their frequency-domain representations, resulting in \({a\_{LL}}(\omega )\), \({a\_{LV}}(\omega )\), \({a\_{RL}}(\omega )\), and \({a\_{RV}}(\omega )\). Subsequently, we can solve for the four unknown loads *F**LL*(*ω*), *F**LV*(*ω*), *F**RL*(*ω*), and *F**RV*(*ω*) using the four linear equations provided in formula ([20](/articles/s41598-025-96212-2#Equ20)) to obtain a unique solution. Once we acquire the frequency-domain data for the left and right wheel rail forces, we can employ inverse Fourier transform according to formula ([17](/articles/s41598-025-96212-2#Equ17)) to obtain the time-domain discrete signals *f**LL*(*n*), *f**LV*(*n*), *f**RL*(*n*) and *f**RV*(*n*) for the left and right lateral vertical wheel rail forces.

This method offers accurate calculations of frequency response functions using finite element models, fully accounting for modal resonance issues, thereby demonstrating excellent performance in solving wheel rail forces in the high-frequency range.

## Results and discussion

### Application of the above high-frequency wheel-rail force calculation method

To confirm and enhance the accuracy of the calculation approach for high-frequency wheel-rail force, we initially constructed a finite element model of the axle box and wheelset, while considering the impact of the rail. Subsequently, we developed an independent rail model. The rails are fixedly constrained at both ends, and the tread shape and material of the rails are consistent with those of actual tracks. This rail model is connected to the wheel via a stiffness damping unit, thereby enhancing the authenticity of the simulation. And the spring, axle box rotary arm positioning node, and primary vertical damper are all simulated using spring elements (as shown in Fig. [1](/articles/s41598-025-96212-2#Fig1)). The other end of these spring units is connected to the ground. The stiffness parameters of each spring unit are strictly set according to actual vehicle parameters, and the specific values can be found in Table [1](/articles/s41598-025-96212-2#Tab1). Finally, we conducted modal analysis, focusing primarily on wheel rail forces below 1000 Hz, thus considering only the first 40 modes. The results of modal analysis are detailed in Table [2](/articles/s41598-025-96212-2#Tab2). Modal frequency is an important parameter in modal analysis and serves as the foundation for dynamic design. It is of great significance for avoiding resonance and maintaining structural stability. In the context of solving wheel-rail forces in this paper, modal frequency influences the magnitude of wheel-rail forces by affecting the frequency response function. Therefore, modal analysis is of great importance.

To obtain an accurate frequency response function, we applied unit lateral and vertical forces at the wheel rail contact positions of the left and right wheels, including unit forces in the real and imaginary parts. This allows us to solve and calculate the frequency response function. After the calculation is complete, we output at the response points of the left and right axle boxes to provide the frequency response function required by formula ([20](/articles/s41598-025-96212-2#Equ20)). It is important to note that all parameters in formula ([20](/articles/s41598-025-96212-2#Equ20)) are in plural form, indicating that they have both real and imaginary parts. Similarly, when applying a unit force, we also consider both the real and imaginary parts. This approach aims to more comprehensively simulate the actual situation and ensure the accuracy of the results.

![figure 1](//media.springernature.com/lw685/springer-static/image/art%3A10.1038%2Fs41598-025-96212-2/MediaObjects/41598_2025_96212_Fig1_HTML.png)

Finite element model of the wheel-to-axle box system.

Figures [2](/articles/s41598-025-96212-2#Fig2), [3](/articles/s41598-025-96212-2#Fig3), [4](/articles/s41598-025-96212-2#Fig4) and [5](/articles/s41598-025-96212-2#Fig5) present the frequency response functions of wheel rail force load and left axle box lateral acceleration, wheel rail force load and left axle box vertical acceleration, wheel rail force load and right axle box lateral acceleration, as well as the relationship curves between wheel rail force load and right axle box vertical acceleration. In each frequency response curve, the load includes the unit force of the vertical real part of the left wheel, the unit force of the vertical imaginary part of the left wheel, the unit force of the horizontal real part of the left wheel, the unit force of the horizontal imaginary part of the left wheel, the unit force of the vertical real part of the right wheel, the unit force of the vertical imaginary part of the right wheel, and the unit force of the horizontal imaginary part of the right wheel.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Field measurements involve direct collection of data on rail vibrations and track conditions using specialized vehicles and accelerometers, as explored in various studies.
Advanced sensor technologies, including instrumented wheelsets and accelerometers, provide precise data on wheel-rail forces and vibrations, enhancing understanding of track performance.
Data analysis tools, like Fourier Transform and finite element modeling, enable detailed interpretation of collected data, facilitating accurate assessments of track dynamics and performance.
